behaviors.description: The conversation history passed to the LLM is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when the full history exceeds the context window.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module handoff: history-selection logic must be wired correctly into the LLM request builder. A pure unit test of the trimming helper can miss bugs where the wrong slice/order is actually sent. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no requirement for real OS/network/subprocess, no dependence on model output quality, and no multi-agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: Wire conversation service + history trimmer + LLM client adapter with an in-process fake LLM transport that captures request payload. Provide >10 prior messages and assert the outbound prompt contains exactly the most recent 10 in correct order.

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real LLM semantic quality/relevance, which is non-deterministic and cannot be validated by deterministic stubs without hiding true failures. Workflow is not required because this behavior evaluates a single generation outcome, not a multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run real model calls on a fixed prompt set of customer questions with conversation context, score relevance via human rubric or calibrated evaluator, and fail when responses are generic/off-topic beyond threshold.

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration.
behaviors.justification: The key failure is tone judgment accuracy ('correctly identifies'), which depends on real model behavior; mocked classifiers can always pass and hide misclassification. Workflow was considered, but disqualified because this behavior only needs one real agentic judgment plus verification that regeneration is triggered, not full multi-step quality validation of the regenerated output.
behaviors.test_description: Feed representative responses for casual-tier users through the real tone-check path and assert too-formal outputs are flagged; verify orchestration issues a regeneration request when flagged (regen call can be asserted via stubbed second-call endpoint).

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is local component wiring and persistence contract (chat flow -> repository -> DB schema/timestamps). This is catchable with a real local DB/filesystem-backed test; no need for external OS/network variability beyond standard local integration. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: persistence correctness does not require LLM quality evaluation or multi-agentic sequencing beyond stored records.
behaviors.test_description: Execute chat flow against a test database, including a forced regeneration path, then query persisted records and assert all artifacts exist (original user input, drafts/regenerations) with expected ordering and timestamp fields/population rules.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft.
behaviors.justification: This is an orchestration/state-exposure contract across local modules (generation, tone gate, response selector/output). Deterministic stubs can force 'reject then regenerate' and verify only final response is returned, so real model quality is not required. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no requirement for real OS/network/subprocess failure mode, and no need for multi-agentic quality checks.
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end service method with stubbed LLM outputs: first reply marked too formal, second acceptable. Assert API/service return payload contains only second reply and that first draft is excluded from customer-facing field while retained only in internal logs if designed.
